title: Lesson Quiz: Managing Spending and Avoiding Financial Pitfalls
description: Assessment of the concept that Overspending, impulse buying, and financial discipline.

choices.text: Making an unplanned purchase without careful evaluation
choices.feedback: Correct. Impulse buying means purchasing quickly without careful comparison or planning.

choices.text: Following a budget exactly as planned
choices.feedback: Following a budget is not impulse buying.
Saving for emergencies is a planned financial strategy, not impulse buying.

choices.text: Choosing a checking account instead of a savings account
choices.feedback: That is an account choice, not a description of impulse buying.

questions.prompt: Overspending can weaken a budget and make it harder to reach financial goals.

choices.feedback: Overspending can reduce money available for needs, savings, and other priorities.
Correct. Overspending can damage a budget and make goals harder to reach.

questions.prompt: A student has $50 left after planned expenses and is considering a $32 unplanned purchase. What is the strongest concern?

choices.feedback: This amount is too low. Subtract the purchase from the $50 remaining carefully.
An unplanned purchase usually reduces available money rather than improving savings.
Correct. The strongest concern is that very little money remains after the purchase.

choices.text: The purchase means budgeting no longer matters
choices.feedback: Budgeting still matters when evaluating whether a purchase is strong or weak.

questions.prompt: Why is financial discipline important in spending decisions?

choices.text: Because every spending decision should focus only on what feels good now
choices.feedback: That ignores long-term priorities. Financial discipline is stronger because it compares short-term wants with long-term consequences.

choices.text: Because spending choices should be based only on emotions
choices.feedback: Strong spending decisions should be based on evidence and consequences, not only emotions.

choices.text: Because all purchases are strong if there is any money available
choices.feedback: A purchase can still be weak even if some money is available.

choices.text: Because it helps compare short-term wants with long-term priorities
choices.feedback: Correct. Financial discipline helps support stronger long-term money decisions.

questions.prompt: A purchase is always a strong decision if it fits mathematically in the budget.

choices.feedback: A purchase can fit mathematically and still be a weak choice if it harms priorities or reduces flexibility too much.
Correct. Strong spending decisions depend on priorities and consequences, not only whether the math works.
